---
title: "DICoP Annual Anomaly Detection"
subtitle: "EMS and Trauma Registry Reporting | 2020–2026"
date: "2026-07-22"
format:
  letterbox-revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
    theme: custom.scss
    logo: figures/HHS_PH_horiz_color_print.jpg
    transition: slide
    menu: true
    slide-number: false
    self-contained: true
    toc: false
    notes: true
    progress: false
    title-slide-attributes:
      class: title-slide
      data-background-color: white
      data-background-size: cover
      data-background-position: center
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  eval: true
  include: false
engine: julia
---

# Introduction

## Project Purpose
* Annual anomaly detection for EMS agencies and trauma centers.
* Use of distributional methods and practical thresholds.
* Overview of data, methodology, and results.

<!-- Setup code -->

```{julia}
#| label: setup

###_____________________________________________________________________________
# Setup for the anomaly detection project to leverage Tidier and DotEnv
# Setup for the environment variables to be used to secure filepaths
###_____________________________________________________________________________

using Pkg

# Ensure required packages are available
Pkg.activate(".");
Pkg.instantiate();

# only need to install packages the first time
Pkg.add(["Tidier", "DotEnv", "XLSX", "DataFrames", "Statistics", "Distributions", "Random", "Plots", "StatsBase", "StatsPlots"]);

# Load packages
using Tidier
using DotEnv
using XLSX
using DataFrames
using Random
using Statistics
using Distributions
using Plots
using StatsBase
using StatsPlots

# get custom functions
include("./Julia/utils.jl");

# Load .env file into ENV[]
DotEnv.load!();

###_____________________________________________________________________________
# Set up paths to data files using environment variables
###_____________________________________________________________________________

## Trauma Registry ----

### counts
iowa_trauma_registry_counts_path = ENV["IOWA_TRAUMA_REGISTRY_DATA_PATH"];

### anomalies
iowa_trauma_registry_anomalies_path = ENV["IOWA_TRAUMA_REGISTRY_ANOMALIES_DATA_PATH"];

## EMS Registry ----

### counts
iowa_ems_registry_counts_path = ENV["IOWA_EMS_REGISTRY_DATA_PATH"];

### anomalies
iowa_ems_registry_anomalies_path = ENV["IOWA_EMS_REGISTRY_ANOMALIES_DATA_PATH"];

```

<!-- Data Setup for Trauma Registry -->

```{julia}
#| label: data-setup-trauma

# load trauma registry counts and differences 
trauma_counts = DataFrame(
  XLSX.readtable(iowa_trauma_registry_counts_path, "data")
  );

# load trauma registry anomalies
trauma_anomalies = DataFrame(
  XLSX.readtable(iowa_trauma_registry_anomalies_path, "anomalies")
  );

# summarize anomalies identified using each strategy for the trauma use case
trauma_anomalies_summary = @chain trauma_counts begin
  @summarize(z_anomaly_total = sum(z_anomaly_2025),
             z_anomaly_only = sum(z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             pct_anomaly_total = sum(pct_anomaly_2025),
             pct_anomaly_only = sum(pct_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_total = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_only = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025),
             total_anomalies = sum(z_anomaly_2025 | pct_anomaly_2025 | nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             total_facilities = n()
             )
  @pivot_longer(
    [z_anomaly_total, z_anomaly_only, pct_anomaly_total, pct_anomaly_only, nb_anomaly_total, nb_anomaly_only], 
    names_to = anomaly_method, 
    values_to = anomaly_count
    )
  @relocate(
    [total_anomalies, total_facilities],
    after = anomaly_count
  )
  @mutate(
    anomaly_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_anomalies * 100; digits=2),
    facility_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_facilities * 100; digits=2),
    overall_anomaly_pct = round(total_anomalies / total_facilities * 100; digits=2),
    anomaly_method = replace.(anomaly_method, r"_total" => "")
  )
end

# recalculate anomalies identified using each strategy for the trauma use case, excluding percent change method
trauma_anomalies_update = @chain trauma_counts begin
  @summarize(z_anomaly_total = sum(z_anomaly_2025),
             z_anomaly_only = sum(z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_total = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_only = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025),
             total_anomalies = sum(z_anomaly_2025 | nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             total_facilities = n()
             )
  @pivot_longer(
    [z_anomaly_total, z_anomaly_only, nb_anomaly_total, nb_anomaly_only], 
    names_to = anomaly_method, 
    values_to = anomaly_count
    )
  @relocate(
    [total_anomalies, total_facilities],
    after = anomaly_count
  )
  @mutate(
    anomaly_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_anomalies * 100; digits=2),
    facility_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_facilities * 100; digits=2),
    overall_anomaly_pct = round(total_anomalies / total_facilities * 100; digits=2),
    anomaly_method = replace.(anomaly_method, r"_total" => "")
  )
end
```

<!-- Data Setup for EMS Data -->

```{julia}
#| label: data-setup-ems

# load ems registry counts and differences
ems_counts = DataFrame(
  XLSX.readtable(iowa_ems_registry_counts_path, "data")
  );

# load ems registry anomalies
ems_anomalies = DataFrame(
  XLSX.readtable(iowa_ems_registry_anomalies_path, "anomalies")
  );

# calculate differences between anomalies identified using each strategy for the ems use case
ems_anomalies_summary = @chain ems_counts begin
  @summarize(z_anomaly_total = sum(z_anomaly_2025),
             z_anomaly_only = sum(z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             pct_anomaly_total = sum(pct_anomaly_2025),
             pct_anomaly_only = sum(pct_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_total = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_only = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025),
             total_anomalies = sum(z_anomaly_2025) + sum(pct_anomaly_2025) + sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             total_agencies = n()
             )
  @pivot_longer(
    [z_anomaly_total, z_anomaly_only, pct_anomaly_total, pct_anomaly_only, nb_anomaly_total, nb_anomaly_only], 
    names_to = anomaly_method, 
    values_to = anomaly_count
    )
  @relocate(
    [total_anomalies, total_agencies],
    after = anomaly_count
  )
  @mutate(
    anomaly_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_anomalies * 100; digits=2),
    agency_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_agencies * 100; digits=2),
    overall_anomaly_pct = round(total_anomalies / total_agencies * 100; digits=2),
    anomaly_method = replace.(anomaly_method, r"_total" => "")
  )
end

# recalculate differences between anomalies identified using each strategy for the ems use case, excluding percent change method
ems_anomalies_summary_update = @chain ems_counts begin
  @summarize(z_anomaly_total = sum(z_anomaly_2025),
             z_anomaly_only = sum(z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025 & !nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_total = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             nb_anomaly_only = sum(nb_pois_anomaly_2025 & !z_anomaly_2025 & !pct_anomaly_2025),
             total_anomalies = sum(z_anomaly_2025 | nb_pois_anomaly_2025),
             total_agencies = n()
             )
  @pivot_longer(
    [z_anomaly_total, z_anomaly_only, nb_anomaly_total, nb_anomaly_only], 
    names_to = anomaly_method, 
    values_to = anomaly_count
    )
  @relocate(
    [total_anomalies, total_agencies],
    after = anomaly_count
  )
  @mutate(
    anomaly_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_anomalies * 100; digits=2),
    agency_pct = round(anomaly_count / total_agencies * 100; digits=2),
    overall_anomaly_pct = round(total_anomalies / total_agencies * 100; digits=2),
    anomaly_method = replace.(anomaly_method, r"_total" => "")
  )
end
```

<!-- Create plots of distributions of interest -->
```{julia}
#| label: dist-setup

plt_norm = simulate_and_plot("normal")
plt_poisson = simulate_and_plot("poisson")
plt_quasipoisson = simulate_and_plot("quasipoisson")
plt_nb = simulate_and_plot("nb")

```

# Data Description

## Source and Structure
* Annual counts of record submission (2020–2026).
* Stable data generating process via scheduled reports from the registries.

## Distributional Characteristics

* Annual EMS and trauma registry counts follow discrete, right‑skewed distributions.
* We use probability distributions to characterize expected reporting patterns at the facility or agency level.
* Emphasis is on the marginal empirical distributions for each reporting entity, not population parameters.

# Probability Distributions

## Important Distributions

- Normal
- Poisson
- Negative Binomial
- Why quasi-Poisson was not used.

 

## Probability Distributions

* A probability distribution describes how a random variable behaves.
* It specifies the possible values a variable may take and the likelihood associated with each value.
* Provides a mathematical structure for uncertainty, variation, and randomness.

::: {#note_1 .notes}

* Emphasize that a distribution is not merely a shape, but a complete probabilistic description of a variable.
* Clarify that analysts do not always need a fitted model; sometimes the distribution is used descriptively or predictively.
* Reinforce that distributions allow quantification of uncertainty, which is essential in epidemiology, trauma registry research, and EMS systems analysis.
* Highlight that parameters define expected behavior. For example, $\mu$ indicates central tendency, $\sigma^2$ governs spread, and $\lambda$ describes count intensity.
* It is helpful to remind the audience that all subsequent anomaly detection rests on selecting a distribution that reflects the empirical process.

:::

 

## Probability Distributions

* It allows analysts to characterize a variable using parameters such as
  - mean ($\mu$) or expected value $E[X]$
  - variance ($\sigma^2$)
  - rate (\lambda)
  - shape parameters $(r, p, \phi)$.

* Supports modeling, inference, simulation, and prediction across scientific and operational domains.



## Probabilty Density Functions (pdf)

* A PDF applies to continuous random variables.
* It describes how probability is distributed over intervals of real numbers.
* It supports probability computations over ranges via integration.

Example notation:

Normal distribution: $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$


::: {#note_2 .notes}

* Stress that a PDF does not give the probability of a single exact value because continuous variables have infinite resolution.
* Emphasize that PDFs describe “density,” not literal probability.
* Mention continuous science examples: physiological measurements, reaction times, environmental exposures.

:::



## Probability Mass Functions (pmf)

* A PMF applies to discrete random variables.
* It assigns probabilities to exact integer values or categories.
* It supports analysis of count outcomes such as incident frequencies or event arrivals.

::: {#note_3 .notes}

* State that PMFs handle variables that cannot take fractional values.
* Clarify that they are appropriate for modeling incident counts, registry submissions, and other non‑continuous phenomena.
* You may mention that discrete distributions naturally support prediction intervals because probabilities accumulate at exact values.

:::



## Probability Mass Functions (pmf)

* Example notation:

Poisson distribution: $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$

Negative Binomial: $X \sim \text{NB}(r, p)$

 

## Quantile Functions

* A quantile function is the inverse of a cumulative distribution function (CDF).
* It maps a probability $p$ in the interval $[0,1]$ to the corresponding value of the random variable.

::: {#note_3 .notes}

* Clarify that quantile functions are central to anomaly detection because they specify what values are expected with specific probabilities.
* Emphasize that quantile functions are distribution‑specific. The shape and dispersion determine how extreme a value must be to exceed a given probability threshold.
* State explicitly that quantile functions allow analysts to identify the 95% and 99% upper bounds, which are often used in surveillance or operational monitoring.
* Stress that quasi‑Poisson does not have a quantile function because it is a quasi‑likelihood model rather than a fully defined distribution, motivating the NB–Poisson approach in this project.

:::



## Quantile Functions

* Quantile functions identify thresholds such as

  - medians $(p=0.50)$,
  - quartiles $(p=0.25, 0.75)$,
  - prediction bounds for future outcomes

* They provide a direct way to translate probability statements into value statements.
* They are essential for constructing prediction intervals, tolerance intervals, risk thresholds, and outlier rules.

 

## The Normal Distribution
Notation: $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$

Key Properties:

* Continuous distribution.
* Symmetric about the mean.
* Variance controls spread.
* Often used when many small, independent influences accumulate.

::: {#note_4 .notes}

* The normal distribution describes a continuous variable that varies symmetrically around a mean \mu with variance \sigma^2. 
* It is used to represent aggregated effects of many small independent influences. It provides a foundation for standardized scoring, confidence intervals, and modeling of continuous measurements.
* Emphasize symmetry, continuous nature, and interpretability. Clarify that many real‑world variables approximate normal behavior when aggregated over time or individuals.

:::



## The Normal Distribution

General Uses:

* Measurement data such as heights, blood pressure, or laboratory values.
* Modeling aggregated effects where the central limit theorem applies.
* Standardized scoring and z‑score diagnostics.

 

## Poisson Distribution
Notation: $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$

Key Properties:

* Discrete, integer‑valued distribution.
* Mean and variance both equal \lambda.
* Models count processes driven by independent events at a constant rate.

::: {#note_5 .notes}

* The Poisson distribution models counts of events that occur independently at a constant rate \lambda. 
* It assumes equidispersion, meaning the mean and variance are both \lambda. It is frequently used for arrival processes, incident tallies, and other independent event counts.
* Emphasize equidispersion and independence of event arrivals. Note that it is foundational for many stochastic count processes.

:::

 

## Poisson Distribution

General Uses:

* Incident arrivals, service calls, or event tallies.
* Modeling photon counts in physical sciences.
* Queueing systems where events occur independently.



## Quasi-Poisson Model
Notation: $\mu = \lambda, \sigma^2 = \phi\lambda$

Key Properties:

* Extends Poisson to allow overdispersion via φ.
* Not a fully specified probability distribution.
* Lacks a PMF, PDF, and quantile function.
* Used primarily in regression contexts.

::: {#note_6 .notes}

* The quasi‑Poisson model extends the Poisson framework to allow variance inflation through a dispersion parameter \phi such that $\sigma^2 = \phi\lambda$. It is not a fully specified probability distribution. It is primarily used within generalized linear modeling contexts when count data exhibit mild overdispersion relative to Poisson assumptions.
* Can be described as a Poisson-Gamma mixture.
* Clarify that quasi‑Poisson is a quasi‑likelihood framework and cannot generate prediction intervals. Useful in modeling, not in probability‑based decision rules.

:::



## Quasi-Poisson Model

General Uses:

* Count regression models with mild overdispersion.
* Situations where Poisson assumptions fail but a full distribution is not required.
* Modeling datasets where variance inflation is present.



## Negative Binomial Distribution
Notation: $X \sim \text{NB}(r, p)$

where r is the number of successes and p is the probability of success.

NB2 Parameterization: $\mu$, $\sigma^2=\mu+\frac{\mu^2}{r}$

Key Properties:

* Discrete distribution with strong right‑skew.
* Appropriate when variance exceeds the mean.
* Robust to overdispersion.

::: {#note_7 .notes}
* The negative binomial distribution models discrete counts with variance greater than the mean. Under the NB2 mean‑variance relationship, the mean is μ and the variance is $\sigma^2 = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{r}$, where r is a shape parameter. 
* An alternative interpretation views r as the number of successes and models the number of failures before the r‑th success with success probability p. It is widely used for overdispersed count processes.
* Highlight flexibility and suitability for heavy‑tailed count data. Emphasize that NB supports quantile‑based predictions and formal probability statements.

:::



## Negative Binomial Distribution

General Uses:

* Event counts with substantial heterogeneity.
* Biological processes with clustering.
* Overdispersed operational or behavioral data.

# Anomaly Detection Methods

## Z‑Score
* Year-over-year differences.
* Approximate normality.



## Percent Change
- Operational thresholding.



## NB–Poisson Prediction Interval
* Distribution-based expected ranges.

# Results

## Summary of Findings
* Facilities flagged by each detector.
* Agreement between detectors.

# Future Extensions

## Alternative Time Scales
* Weekly or monthly modeling.
* Regression-based anomaly detection.

# Appendix

## Julia Code Examples

```julia
# snippets included here
```
